metadata.title: Research the Three-Validity Hierarchy for Liquid Biopsy with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Ciao, Liam — in for Bear. That is not the same as the test helped? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: That is not the same as the test helped?
props.segment: Research the Three-Validity Hierarchy for Liquid

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. The test detected it. That is not the same as the test helped.

A liquid biopsy company reports forty-five percent of cancer patients carry an actionable alteration. A colleague concludes nearly half could get targeted therapy. What is missing from that sentence? Detecting an alteration and proving the test improves patient outcomes are separated by two validity levels that the headline skips entirely.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — problem card: 45% actionable vs the three-validity gap the headline ignores

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to apply the three-validity framework to three ctDNA use cases and rank them by current clinical utility evidence.

props.command:
claude "Apply the three-validity framework (analytic,
clinical, clinical utility) to three uses of
circulating tumor DNA: (1) EGFR T790M resistance
monitoring in lung cancer, (2) the DYNAMIC trial
MRD-guided adjuvant de-escalation in stage II CRC,
(3) multi-cancer early detection in asymptomatic
people. For each: validity established, utility
evidence pending, rank by current utility evidence."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read it. The script maps each test against all three gates — analytic validity, clinical validity, and clinical utility — and marks pass or fail. EGFR T790M passes all three. DYNAMIC MRD passes all three. MCED passes analytic and clinical validity but stops at utility — no mortality endpoint yet.

props.code:
# validity_framework.py -- Claude Code output
TESTS = [
  {"name": "EGFR T790M (osimertinib)",
   "analytic": True,   # ddPCR validated, CE-marked
   "clinical": True,   # predicts osimertinib response
   "utility":  True,   # FDA approved companion dx; OS benefit
   "note": "Rank 1: all three gates cleared"},
  {"name": "DYNAMIC MRD (stage II CRC)",
   "analytic": True,   # validated ctDNA assays
   "clinical": True,   # MRD+ predicts recurrence
   "utility":  True,   # non-inferior RFS, less chemo
   "note": "Rank 2: utility via non-inferiority, not OS"},
  {"name": "MCED (asymptomatic screening)",
   "analytic": True,   # methylation signal validated
   "clinical": True,   # detects 50+ cancer types
   "utility":  False,  # no mortality endpoint yet
   "note": "Rank 3: gate 3 pending"},
]
for t in TESTS:
    gates = [t['analytic'], t['clinical'], t['utility']]
    print(f"{t['name']}: {['PASS','FAIL'][not all(gates)]}")
    print(f"  {t['note']}")

beats.narration_text: The grid. Three tests, three gates. EGFR T790M: pass, pass, pass — mortality endpoint cleared, drug approved. DYNAMIC MRD: pass, pass, pass — non-inferior RFS with reduced chemotherapy. MCED: pass, pass, fail — detection improved, mortality endpoint pending. The third gate is the only one that matters for patients.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: B04_ValidityFunnel — three-gate grid with tests as rows, pass/fail marks filling in gate by gate

beats.narration_text: Now add the CHIP confounder. Ask Claude how clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential affects any ctDNA result in patients over sixty.

props.command:
claude "Add a cross-cutting caveat: how does clonal
hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP)
affect the interpretation of any ctDNA result in
patients over 60? Which of the three test types
(T790M, DYNAMIC, MCED) is most vulnerable to CHIP
confounding and why?"

beats.narration_text: CHIP is clonal hematopoiesis — blood cells carrying somatic mutations that look like tumor signal but are not. It occurs in over ten percent of people over sixty-five. MCED is most vulnerable because it detects methylation patterns across cancer types, and CHIP-derived signal can mimic early cancer signatures. The third validity gate becomes even harder to clear.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — CHIP caveat applied across all three test types; MCED flagged as most vulnerable

beats.narration_text: Analytical sophistication is not clinical utility. The test that detects the most is not automatically the test that helps the most. Three validity levels separate a working assay from a proven clinical intervention. Only one of the three ctDNA use cases has cleared all three gates without an asterisk.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: analytic sophistication ≠ utility; three gates; only T790M has all three unambiguously

beats.narration_text: Before ordering any liquid biopsy, ask which validity level has been established for this specific indication. Analytic validity means the assay works in the lab. Clinical validity means the result predicts something real. Clinical utility means acting on the result changes what happens to the patient. Three different questions, three different answers.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps: three-question checklist before ordering any liquid biopsy

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Analytical sophistication is not clinical utility. The test that detects the most is not automatically the test that helps the most. Three validity levels separate a working assay from a proven clinical intervention. Only one of the three ctDNA use cases has cleared all three gates without an asterisk. Before ordering any liquid biopsy, ask which validity level has been established for this specific indication. Analytic validity means the assay works in the lab. Clinical validity means the result predicts something real. Clinical utility means acting on the result changes what happens to the patient. Three different questions, three different answers.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research the Three-Validity Hierarchy for Liquid Biopsy with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
